data.title: Tencent fires WeChat manager after 7-figure bonus leaks online, sparks uproar
data.description:
Chinese tech giant Tencent Holdings has fired a manager at its WeChat division after details of his multimillion-yuan bonus package leaked online, sparking animated discussion across social media.
The employee, surnamed Ye, was dismissed for passing sensitive corporate information to external parties, which caused a “severe negative impact both inside and outside the company”, according to an internal company bulletin seen by three Tencent employees.
Ye served as a project manager within Tencent’s Weixin Group – the core engine behind WeChat, China’s ubiquitous super-app with 1.3 billion users – according to the notice issued by the firm’s Anti-Corruption Investigation Department.
Tencent did not immediately respond to a request for comment on Friday.
The dismissal came after a screenshot detailing Ye’s staggering annual pay began circulating widely on Chinese social media. The image – which appears to be taken from a company platform – revealed the manager had a compensation package worth roughly 3.17 million yuan (US$470,000).
That included a cash bonus of more than 820,000 yuan and stock incentives worth over 2.35 million yuan, according to the screenshot.
The internal bulletin states that Ye has not only been fired, but also permanently blacklisted from future employment at Tencent. The company will release a formal circular later detailing the disciplinary measures it will take against him over his breach of executive conduct, it adds.
Tencent has been doubling down on internal discipline in recent years.
In an annual corporate report released in January, the Shenzhen-based firm disclosed that its anti-corruption unit had uncovered more than 70 compliance violations in 2025. The probes resulted in the dismissal of more than 90 employees, with over 20 handed to law enforcement for criminal prosecution.
Tencent’s compliance rules prohibit staff from disclosing sensitive corporate information including trade secrets, internal communications, unreleased source code and employee records.
Additional reporting by Minxiao Chang

data.title: Hong Kong police officer removed from duties after swearing at passer-by
data.description:
A Hong Kong police officer has been removed from duties after he repeatedly swore at a passer-by with a loudspeaker in a patrol car, a source has said.
According to a video clip circulated on social media on Friday, a police car marked “AM 7155” on its roof arrived at Cho Yiu Chuen housing estate in Lai King with its siren on.
Someone inside the vehicle then appeared to use the on-board loudspeaker to shout abusive words at a passer-by dressed in white clothing.
The passer-by immediately responded with a barrage of curses, and profanities were heard again over the police car’s loudspeaker, leading to a heated verbal exchange between the two sides in the middle of the night.
A source told the South China Morning Post that the officer involved had been removed from duties.
“A disciplinary investigation has been initiated immediately to strictly follow up with the matter,” the source said.
The SCMP learned that before the incident, the officer involved had handled a dispute between a 38-year-old taxi driver and a 61-year-old passenger in the area on Thursday night. The passenger had reportedly accused the driver of taking too long to give him his change.
Another video clip circulated online showed both the driver and the passenger outside the taxi, with the passenger hurling profanities at the driver and preventing him from returning to his vehicle.
The police officer involved in the loudspeaker incident had arrested the passenger at his home on Friday morning, according to the source.

data.title: Chinese GPU start-up MetaX confidentially files for Hong Kong listing: sources
data.description:
MetaX, a Chinese maker of graphics processing units (GPUs), has confidentially filed for a listing in Hong Kong, according to people familiar with the matter.
The Shanghai-based company was targeting an initial public offering (IPO) by the end of this year, according to the sources, who asked not to be identified because the information was confidential.
MetaX was working with Huatai International Financial Holdings on the potential share sale, the people said.
The company did not immediately respond to a request for comment on Friday.
The IPO plan comes amid a surge of capital-raising by China’s artificial intelligence chipmakers, with peers Biren Technology, Iluvatar CoreX and Moore Threads listing in Shanghai or Hong Kong since late 2025.
The wave has been driven by Beijing’s semiconductor self-reliance initiative amid US export controls.
Founded in 2020, MetaX has emerged as one of China’s most closely watched GPU start-ups. It has attracted investors partly because its founding team includes former AMD executives, as domestic companies race to develop alternatives to Nvidia’s AI chips.
MetaX announced in June that it would pursue a Hong Kong listing, less than half a year after its debut on Shanghai’s technology-focused Star Market. The company raised 4.2 billion yuan (US$620 million), with shares soaring 685 per cent since its IPO.
The company said in June that the proposed H-share offering would support its business expansion, strengthen corporate governance and competitiveness, and advance its international growth strategy.
The plan calls for issuing H shares equivalent to no more than 5 per cent of its enlarged share capital following the offering, before any overallotment option is exercised.
The listing is also expected to reinforce Hong Kong’s status as a fundraising hub for China’s tech champions, following a string of landmark debuts earlier this year.
In January, Biren Technology raised HK$5.58 billion (US$711 million), with shares surging 87 per cent to date. Iluvatar CoreX, which went public a few days later, raised around HK$3.7 billion and has surged nearly 290 per cent.
Kunlunxin, the AI chip unit of Chinese tech giant Baidu, is also pursuing a Hong Kong listing. The company submitted a confidential IPO filing in January, and was seeking a valuation of at least 100 billion yuan, the South China Morning Post reported in May.

data.title: China’s Kimi K3 well behind US rivals in cyberattack ability, study shows
data.description:
Chinese unicorn Moonshot AI’s Kimi K3 model trails far behind top American rivals in its ability to launch cyberattacks, joint British-US government research shows, challenging Washington’s brewing anxiety over the rapid rise of Chinese open-source artificial intelligence.
The model, currently considered China’s most powerful large language model, performs “significantly below the most recent frontier cyber-capable models”, according to a report published on Thursday by the UK Artificial Intelligence Security Institute (AISI) and the US Centre for AI Standards and Innovation (CAISI).
The AISI is a research arm under the UK Department for Science, Innovation and Technology, while the CAISI operates within the US Department of Commerce’s National Institute of Standards and Technology.
To evaluate capabilities, the two government bodies put Kimi K3 through ExploitBench, a public benchmark assessing an AI’s ability to develop exploits for cybersecurity vulnerabilities.
Kimi K3 achieved an overall score of 32.2 per cent, outperforming domestic rival Zhipu AI’s GLM-5.2 at 24.4 per cent, but lagging well behind top, unnamed US models that averaged 76.2 per cent.
Notably, Kimi K3 failed to achieve arbitrary code execution – the highest-level exploit granting full control of a target system – across all 41 ExploitBench tasks, whereas leading US models achieved it on 20 tasks.

The model showed similar gaps on The Last Ones, a private 32-step corporate network attack simulation from AISI that typically takes a human expert 20 hours to complete. Kimi K3 reached an average of 17 steps, compared to 28.5 steps achieved by top US models, the report said.
Despite the performance gap, the Chinese model successfully completed the simulated network attack on one of its 10 attempts, showing that it was “capable of autonomously attacking small, weakly defended and vulnerable enterprise systems”, the report noted.
Previously, only four publicly released closed-weight models had ever solved the benchmark.
The findings offer a stark reality check amid mounting panic in Washington over Beijing’s AI advancements following Moonshot AI’s latest flagship release.
Kimi K3 raised alarm bells in the West after outperforming Anthropic’s top-tier Claude Fable 5 and OpenAI’s GPT-5.6 Sol on Arena AI’s benchmark for front-end web development tasks. It also nearly caught up with GPT-5.6 Sol’s cybersecurity performance in tests run by Belgian firm Aikido Security.
The rapid rise of open-weight models from China led Dean Ball, head of strategic futures at OpenAI, to warn of a “dystopian hellscape” driven by “full AI communism”.
Tensions escalated further on Wednesday when Washington accused Chinese firms of model “distillation” – an industry practice of using outputs from larger models to train smaller ones.
Some US officials alleged Kimi K3 distilled Anthropic’s Fable 5 and stole American intellectual property, with US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent threatening sanctions on Chinese AI firms.
The UK-US evaluation suggests fears over Chinese model supremacy may be overstated, according to David Sacks, former White House AI tsar and current co-chair of US President Donald Trump’s Council of Advisers on Science and Technology, who urged American policymakers to avoid over-regulating the sector.
“The Kimi Panic needs to stop,” Sacks said in a social media post. “American frontier models are still ahead.”

data.title: As weight-loss drug orders surge, global funds pile into China’s WuXi AppTec
data.description:
US global asset managers, including BlackRock, have expanded stakes in mainland Chinese pharmaceutical contractor WuXi AppTec over the past two months, betting that a surge in weight-loss drug manufacturing orders will lift earnings in the first half of the year.
Global investors were increasingly interested in China’s contract development and manufacturing organisation, and biotechnology sector, said Linda Shu, head of China healthcare research at HSBC.
“WuXi Apptec is heavily owned given its high expectations” for the first half, Shu said.
JPMorgan Chase remained one of its largest shareholders after raising its stake to 11.39 per cent on July 20, up from 10.98 per cent, according to the Hong Kong stock exchange website.
Swiss banking giant UBS Group and BlackRock are also substantial shareholders, which refer to any investor holding an interest of 5 per cent or more in the voting shares of a listed company.
UBS bought 255,500 shares at an average price of HK$121.51, lifting its stake to 8.02 per cent on June 11. BlackRock purchased 1.52 million shares at an average price of HK$153, increasing its stake to 5.21 per cent on May 14.
WuXi AppTec was expected to release its half-year earnings results on August 3, according to the company. Its Hong Kong-traded stock jumped about 37 per cent over the period, bucking the broad decline in the Hang Seng Index.
Nomura said it expected the leading Chinese drug contractor to report a 10.9 per cent year-on-year earnings growth in the first six months.
WuXi AppTec was an “important” player globally in researching, developing and manufacturing diabetes and weight-loss drugs targeting gut hormone GLP-1, said Zhang Jialin, head of China healthcare research at Nomura.
The company was producing 23 programmes tied to GLP-1, accounting for about 26 per cent of the global late-stage pipeline, Zhang said.
GLP-1 treatments are surging in the Chinese market. Semaglutide, Novo Nordisk’s blockbuster drug which generated about US$34.6 billion in global sales last year, was added in July to China’s state-backed National Essential Drug List.
Eli Lilly’s tirzepatide was included in December on China’s national medical insurance reimbursement list, the state insurance scheme that swaps deep discounts for big volume.
As part of efforts to curb China’s rise in the global pharmaceutical supply chain, the US added WuXi AppTec to the military list, a designation that could restrict cross-border cooperation between US pharmaceutical companies and the Chinese drug manufacturer.
“Some multinational pharmaceutical giants are having to reshuffle their supply chains away from China amid US-China tensions,” said Geoffrey Hsu, a general partner at the US-based investment firm OrbiMed, in a recent interview.
“They don’t want to be dependent upon China to supply drugs to the United States,” he said, referring to Washington’s concerns over drug supply security.
Hsu described decoupling as difficult and costly, adding it could take five to 10 years to gradually shift manufacturing away from China.

data.title: Hong Kong exchange’s biggest reform in 8 years opens IPO gates
data.description:
Stock exchange operator Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing (HKEX) will allow all listing applications to remain confidential and will reduce market-capitalisation requirements for listings by start-ups and international firms immediately, it announced on Friday.
Confirming a report by the South China Morning Post, HKEX will proceed with confidential filing as it proposed in March in its biggest set of listing reforms since 2018.
The exchange will also reduce the market-cap requirements for weighted voting right (WVR) companies to HK$20 billion (US$2.6 billion), from HK$40 billion currently. It will also lower the thresholds for a company using the revenue test to HK$6 billion in market capitalisation and HK$600 million in revenue in the most recent financial year, compared with HK$10 billion in market cap and HK$1 billion in revenue now.
While some respondents in a consultation process requested lower thresholds, HKEX said most respondents agreed with the chosen reductions, and the proposal in March was in line with the levels in Shanghai and Shenzhen. The exchange received 73 responses during the consultation from March to May.
The market cap requirement for overseas-listed innovative companies launching secondary listings in Hong Kong will also be lowered to HK$6 billion from HK$10 billion.
The reforms aim to attract more innovative companies from around the world to list in Hong Kong.
HKEX, which was the top global exchange in terms of initial public offering (IPO) fundraising last year, lost its crown to Nasdaq in the first half of 2026, a change brought about by last month’s blockbuster US$75 billion IPO by Elon Musk’s SpaceX.
The confidential filing mechanism will allow candidates to disclose only certain details after their listings are approved by the exchange operator, matching regulations in most other markets including the US, UK and Singapore.
The change will end a rule in place since October 2013 that requires all listing candidates to publish their listing documents – including corporate history, business models and financial information – on the HKEX website once they submit a listing application.
The obligation was enacted to provide more information to investors and research analysts early in the listing process, but start-ups have raised concerns about revealing such information to competitors.
The reduced market capitalisation level for WVR companies will narrow the gap between Hong Kong and other markets. The New York Stock Exchange requires US$750 million, the UK US$39.7 million and Singapore US$116 million.
Global stock exchanges are fighting to attract innovative companies with WVR structures, because many founders, having diluted their stakes to raise early-stage funding, no longer hold a majority of shares.

data.title: Shifting priorities: how affluent buyers fuel Hong Kong’s record insurance sales
data.description:
Life insurance sales in Hong Kong rose 51 per cent in the first quarter to another record high, driven by affluent customers from mainland China and overseas buying policies for wealth transfer, protection and medical needs.
The industry wrote HK$141.1 billion (US$18 billion) in new life policies in the first quarter, compared with HK$93.4 billion a year earlier, according to data from the Insurance Authority released on Friday.
It marked the third year that first-quarter sales hit a record high since the authority was established in 2016.
Demand for products addressing longevity among Hongkongers was a key driver, said Wilton Kee Wing-tao, CEO of Manulife Hong Kong and Macau.
“Customers are increasingly planning for longer, healthier and more financially secure lives, while rising demand for wealth accumulation, legacy and intergenerational wealth planning has further strengthened the appeal of insurance solutions,” Kee said.
“These shifting priorities are converging with Hong Kong’s rising stature as a leading global financial centre and family office hub, creating a strong platform for insurers to help customers plan across life stages and generations.”
In December, Manulife’s Hong Kong unit became the first major global insurer to redomicile from Bermuda to the city to capture growing opportunities.
High-net-worth individuals continue to be a key driver for demand in insurance, as they seek solutions that can simultaneously help grow, preserve and pass on wealth to future generations
Daisy Tsang, CEO of HSBC Life Hong Kong and Macau
Hong Kong had more than 3,380 single-family offices at the end of 2025, an increase of about 680 over the two years, according to a study by the Hong Kong Institute for Monetary and Financial Research.
HSBC Life remained the market leader in new life insurance sales in the first quarter, with market share at 26.6 per cent, according to Insurance Authority data. The unit of HSBC Holdings has held the top spot for the past four years.
Daisy Tsang, CEO of HSBC Life Hong Kong and Macau, said the insurer had attracted customers from 50 nationalities, underscoring the city’s appeal to international visitors from Asia and the Middle East seeking wealth management solutions.
“International money continues to flow in, particularly from the wealth segment, supported by the growing number of family offices establishing themselves in Hong Kong,” she said.
Visitor arrivals rose 17 per cent to 14 million in the first quarter, with 77 per cent coming from mainland China, according to data from the Hong Kong Tourism Board.
Many visitors are buying insurance and wealth products during their trips, according to insurers.
“High-net-worth individuals continue to be a key driver for demand in insurance, as they seek solutions that can simultaneously help grow, preserve and pass on wealth to future generations,” Tsang said.
“Insurance is poised to play a critical role for Hong Kong as it continues to establish itself as a leading financial and risk management hub, especially with its increasing relevance for supporting ageing populations.”

data.title: Cathay to raise passenger fuel surcharges by up to 41% amid Middle East tensions
data.description:
Hong Kong flag carrier Cathay Pacific Airways will raise fuel surcharges by up to 41 per cent on most passenger flights from August, citing escalating tensions in the Middle East.
The airline said on Friday that the higher passenger and cargo fuel surcharges would take effect on August 1, following a brief period of declines after prices peaked in April.
“This is the first increase to passenger and cargo fuel surcharges since April, and levels remain below their peak in that month,” Cathay Pacific said.
Citing data from the International Air Transport Association (IATA), Cathay noted that the global average jet fuel price rose to US$149.40 per barrel for the week ending July 16, up from US$127.06 the previous week and US$119.13 in the week ending July 3.
According to the airline, fuel surcharges on flights between Hong Kong and the South Asian subcontinent will experience the steepest rise, jumping by 41.3 per cent from HK$448 (US$57) to HK$633.
Surcharges on routes between Hong Kong and mainland China will increase by 20 per cent.
For flights departing from the city to the mainland, the cost will rise from HK$165 to HK$198, while the surcharge for routes going in the opposite direction would increase from 135 yuan (US$19.90) to 162 yuan.

For long-haul flights between the city and the South West Pacific, North America, Europe, the Middle East or Africa, the surcharge will rise by about 41.1 per cent, going from HK$965 to HK$1,362.
Charges for other routes will also rise by 40.7 per cent to HK$339.
Meanwhile, cargo flights are for an even bigger increase. The surcharge for short-haul flights will jump from HK$1.80 per kilogram to HK$2.80 – an increase of 55.6 per cent, while the extra cost for medium- and long-haul flights will rise by about 50 and 50.7 per cent, respectively.
Cathay said there had been multiple reductions in fuel surcharges since May as jet fuel prices eased, demonstrating the mechanism’s responsiveness to both downward and upward price movements.
“We continue to review and revise fuel surcharges every two weeks,” the airline said.
Tensions between the United States and Iran have escalated this month after a June ceasefire collapsed.
The US has carried out a series of air strikes on Iran, while the Strait of Hormuz has been nearly closed, pushing up global oil prices.

data.title: HSBC agrees to US$2 billion deal to sell Singapore insurance branch to Allianz
data.description:
HSBC has agreed to sell its Singapore life and health insurance business to Germany’s Allianz for S$2.7 billion (US$2.08 billion), with the deal expected to be completed in the first half of 2027, HSBC Group announced on Friday morning before the start of trading.
The disposal is expected to generate a pre-tax gain of US$1.8 billion and boost HSBC’s common equity tier 1 ratio by up to 15 basis points, the bank said.
As part of the deal, HSBC Singapore will enter a 15-year exclusive bancassurance distribution agreement with Allianz, receiving an initial S$200 million cash payment for distributing insurance products to its retail and high-net-worth clients.
Completion remains subject to regulatory approval. If finalised, Allianz will own HSBC Life Singapore and retain all of its employees.
This transaction is part of HSBC’s ongoing efforts to streamline its business, focusing on areas with strong competitive edges to boost revenue and market share.
The sale marks a reversal of HSBC's approach to its Singapore insurance business. HSBC’s insurance arm expanded in Singapore in 2022 when it purchased French rival AXA for US$575 million, consolidated more than 600,000 active policies covering life and death, health and property in Singapore, the bank said in the 2021 acquisition announcement.
HSBC remains committed to supporting Singapore as an international wealth and wholesale banking hub, the bank said.

Recently, HSBC has been shrinking its global footprint to boost profitability, while growing its wealth products in Asia and the Middle East.
“Singapore serves as [Allianz’s] Asia-Pacific headquarters and is central to our global growth strategy. Allianz’s expansion in Singapore underscores our resolve to help more people meet their protection, health, retirement and wealth needs globally,” said Oliver Bate, the CEO of Allianz SE in a statement.
HSBC Life Singapore generated an operating profit of 80 million euros (US$91.07 million) in 2025 and holds 1.2 billion euros in comprehensive equity, according to the Allianz statement.
HSBC’s share price fell 1.2 per cent to HK$159.7 at the midday pause on Friday after the announcement of the deal, in line with the Hang Seng Index which fell 1.3 per cent at the same time.
“HSBC has been restructuring its business by disposing of the non-core business units,” said Tom Chan Pak-lam, honorary president of the Institute of Securities Dealers. “While the bank is a leading life insurance company in Hong Kong, its Singapore insurance unit is relatively small.”
“It would be better for HSBC to sell the Singapore life insurance at a good price ... The deal is positive to the bank’s share price outlook,” he added.
In May, HSBC sold its retail and wealth management operations in Indonesia to the Oversea-Chinese Banking Corporation, the second-largest banking group in Southeast Asia. In September 2025, it exited both Malta’s and Sri Lanka’s retail banking sectors. Earlier last year, it sold its private bank in Germany and an insurance business in France, and it decided to exit investment banking in Europe and the US.
HSBC CEO Georges Elhedery last year unveiled his plan to exit from “low-return” areas and focus on high-growth businesses in Hong Kong, mainland China, India and other Asian markets.
HSBC has increased its investments in Hong Kong, completing a US$14 billion privatisation of Hang Seng Bank in January, while adding a number of wealth management centres to capture high-net-worth customers.

data.title: Woman arrested over power theft after Hong Kong flat fire kills 5 pets
data.description:
A Hong Kong woman has been arrested for allegedly stealing electricity after a fire broke out in her Mong Kok flat, killing three cats and two dogs.
Investigators said on Friday that the 56-year-old, surnamed Chan, had left her Tung Choi Street flat at about 6pm the previous day with the air conditioner switched on. The flat was believed to have caught fire due to a short circuit.
She was apprehended after checks by power supplier CLP found irregularities in the flat’s electricity supply.
Police were alerted by a security guard at 9.27pm when the blaze broke out. Firefighters forced entry and extinguished the fire.
The animals were found unconscious and staff from the Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals confirmed their deaths at about 11pm.
About 50 people were evacuated. No human injuries were reported.

data.title: Zhongji sets highest subscription threshold in Hong Kong IPO historyZhongji sets an IPO city record for minimum lot price, which may price out retail investors though institutional demand remains stro
data.description:
Zhongji Innolight, the largest IPO in Hong Kong in nearly seven years, has set the highest subscription threshold in the city’s history, dampening retail investors’ appetite, though institutional demand remains strong, with multiple oversubscriptions already placed, according to people familiar with the matter.
Priced at a maximum of HK$1,010 per share and requiring 50 shares per board lot, the minimum threshold is HK$51,009.29 (US$6505.49), making it the most expensive initial public offering entry threshold in Hong Kong’s history.
The company began its Hong Kong public offering on Wednesday, offering 54.5 million H-shares globally, with 10 per cent allocated to retail investors.
As the city’s largest IPO since 2019, Zhongji had successfully attracted substantial institutional interest, with its international placing already oversubscribed, according to sources.
Stocks are traded in so-called “lots” in the city, with the Hong Kong Exchanges & Clearing recently standardising board lot sizes into eight tiers – 1, 50, 100, 500, 1,000, 2,000, 5,000, and 10,000 shares – in order to lower barriers for retail investors, reduce odd lots and boost market liquidity.
High entry thresholds are also seen in nearby markets. In Taiwan, Hon. Precision, an integrated circuit test handler solutions provider, set a record in December with an issue price of NT$1,495, requiring NT$1.495 million per lot.
Meanwhile, Zhongji A-shares require 100 shares per lot, exceeding 100,000 yuan (US$14,769.11) at current market prices.

In contrast, US stock markets do not enforce board lot minimums, allowing investors to buy single shares. However, US IPO allocations follow a pro-rata rule based on individual subscription amounts relative to total market demand.
Alvin Poon, an associate director at Prudential Brokerage, a local brokerage specialising in IPO margin financing, highlighted three main factors that were dampening retail appetite: high entry costs discouraging short-term traders, a narrow A-share price discount of less than 20 per cent, and recent cooling in semiconductor-related stocks and overall IPO sentiment.
Margin financing data from major brokerages showed retail subscription funds exceeded HK$22 billion (US$2.8 billion) by the second day, representing an oversubscription of more than three times that of the retail tranche target.
Zhongji has secured 33 cornerstone investors, including Temasek, Abu Dhabi Investment Authority (ADIA), Alibaba Group Holding, which owns the South China Morning Post, and Tencent Holdings. They have committed over HK$27 billion, accounting for almost half of the total IPO fundraising proceeds.
The public offering will close on Monday, with official trading scheduled to begin on July 30. Goldman Sachs, China International Capital Corporation, Morgan Stanley and GF Securities are serving as joint sponsors.

data.title: Thousands of fans throng 5-day animation, comics and games fair in Hong Kong
data.description:
Thousands of visitors have flocked to an animation, comics and games fair in Hong Kong, drawn to Hello Kitty plush toys, Spider-Man figurines and spinning top contests, among other collectibles and interactive activities.
The five-day Ani-Com & Games Hong Kong fair opened on Friday at the Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre in Wan Chai, with many attendees seeking to buy limited-edition merchandise arriving early in the morning before opening hours.
The first visitor to enter this year’s convention was 21-year-old university student Eric Yip, who arrived at 7am after being selected in a lucky draw as one of the first 300 visitors. He bought a standard HK$50 (US$6.40) admission ticket.
“I was really happy and couldn’t believe I was selected,” he said.
Yip, who has attended the annual event almost every year, said he was prepared to spend more than HK$1,000 on merchandise. He was among the first visitors to get his hands on Sanrio products, including Pompompurin and Cinnamoroll items.
Hongkongers Verna Liu and Kasa Liu, both in their 20s, also came specifically for Sanrio merchandise. The pair paid an additional HK$100 for fast track tickets and waited in line for more than an hour before entering.
They said they were especially looking forward to Sanrio’s long-legged Hello Kitty products, which were only available through limited sales channels.
“I am looking forward to the long-legged Hello Kitty products here because this is a Hong Kong-exclusive collection, and there are only two chances each year to get these products, one online and one here at the exhibition,” Liu said.
Beyond character merchandise, exhibitors said collectible products and interactive experiences were among this year’s highlights.
Connie Chan, head of commerce at Sakasaka, a trading card distributor with six booths at the exhibition, said trading cards were gaining popularity and that this was the first time her company had introduced such products at the fair. =

It also set up a designated area to teach visitors how to participate in an interactive display that projected their Pokemon cards onto screens so they could take photographs.
Beyblade X, appearing for the first time at the show, also attracted young fans with an interactive battle area, where participants could pit their specially designed spinning tops against one another.
Jack Siu, assistant supervisor on Beyblade’s marketing team, said the renewed popularity of the toy reflected consumers’ growing interest in retro products.

data.title: Monkeying around disrupts Hong Kong MTR services for second day in row
data.description:
A monkey caused fresh disruption to Hong Kong’s rail services on Friday morning after it was spotted on the tracks in Tsuen Wan station, a day after a similar incident during peak hours.
At 10.03am, the MTR Corporation said services on the Tsuen Wan line had to be adjusted after a monkey entered the track area, advising passengers to allow extra time for their journeys.
A photo released by the railway operator showed the monkey sitting on the tracks, facing dozens of passengers on the platform.
Officers from the Agriculture, Fisheries and Conservation Department, along with police, later removed the animal. Services resumed about 10 minutes later.
A South China Morning Post reporter observed that announcements about an “animal on the track” were played almost continuously in MTR stations.
On Thursday morning, a monkey made its way onto the tracks near Kowloon Bay station during peak hours, prompting trains on the Kwun Tong line to run at reduced speeds and extending journey times by five to eight minutes.
Additional reporting by Connor Mycroft

data.title: Typhoon Noul to come closest to Hong Kong late Saturday into Sunday
data.description:
Typhoon Noul is expected to come closest to Hong Kong between Saturday night and Sunday morning, according to the city’s weather forecaster, which is considering issuing the No 1 warning signal later on Friday.
The tropical storm near the Philippines was officially named Typhoon Noul. The name, provided by North Korea, means “glowing crimson skies”.
As of Friday morning, Noul was about 500km (310 miles) northeast of Manila and moving northwest at 26km/h (16mph), according to the latest track forecast.
“[Noul] is expected to move across the Luzon Strait and enter within 800km of Hong Kong later on Friday,” said Yeung Hon-yin, a senior scientific officer at the Hong Kong Observatory.
“At that point, the Observatory will consider issuing the No 1 typhoon signal.”
Yeung said the storm was forecast to reach the northeastern part of the South China Sea near the coast of eastern Guangdong over the weekend, when it would be closest to Hong Kong.
He added that Noul could make landfall between Shantou and Huizhou, but its distance from Hong Kong remained uncertain.
Friday will be mostly sunny and hot, with isolated showers. Temperatures are expected to reach 34 degrees Celsius (93.2 Fahrenheit), and may be higher in the New Territories.
Hot conditions will persist on Saturday, but winds will gradually strengthen and squally showers will increase as the typhoon edges closer. The Observatory will assess whether higher warning signals are needed.
Yeung warned of swells and urged the public to stay away from shorelines and suspend water activities.

data.title: Investors fret as China’s A-share divorces echo Bezos’ Amazon split
data.description:
China’s A-share market has seen another high-profile divorce case result in a massive asset split, involving the transfer of 6 billion yuan (US$886 million) – the highest this year – and raising concerns over corporate governance stability and share price fluctuations.
Although the scope of the divorce settlement is not at all comparable to that of Jeff Bezos or Bill Gates, it has made tens of thousands of retail investors worry about their portfolio holdings and paper wealth.
Maxone Semiconductor Suzhou Co, the first domestic probe card maker listed on Shanghai’s Sci-Tech Innovation Board, said in an exchange filing last week that its president, Zhou Ming, 53, had completed all the legal procedures and divided his shareholdings following his divorce.
Zhou transferred half of his personal equity holdings in the company, or 10.86 per cent of the total issued shares, to his ex-wife. Based on calculations after Wednesday’s closing share price, the equity division is worth about 6 billion yuan each.
The announcement was made less than one year after the company’s initial public offering in December.
Shares of Maxone have jumped nearly threefold since its debut, rising to a record intraday high of 671 yuan per share on July 1. But in line with recent tech stock declines, it had already dropped to 417 yuan per share on Thursday.
Zhou’s divorce is just the latest such case reported in China’s A-share market, where retail investors often worry about shares being dumped at high prices following the completion of legal proceedings.
“Tech stocks are volatile at this stage, making the share price reaction hard to gauge,” said a retail investor on Xueqiu, a Beijing-based online social and trading platform last week.
Fu Yifu, a special research fellow at Su Merchants Bank based in Nanjing, eastern Jiangsu province, said spouses could receive substantial shareholdings after a divorce is finalised.
“While share disposals are subject to the China Securities Regulatory Commission (CSRC) and bourse rules, investors still worry about concentrated selling once the shares become tradeable, which could weigh on the stock price,” Fu said.

“Retail investors’ concerns stem from a deep distrust of corporate governance stability and past cases. Listed firms need greater transparency and consistency in their disclosures to ease such worries,” he added.
Shenzhen-listed Trust & Far Technology, a general contractor for data centre information technology infrastructure services, also reported last week that its founder and largest shareholder Zhan Lixiong, 64, has been sued for divorce by his wife, who is requesting a division of their marital assets.
Zhan holds a 20.73 per cent share in the company.
The court has accepted the case, but a hearing has not been scheduled yet, and the amount involved is still to be determined, according to a company statement.
The lawsuit only concerned the shareholder’s personal rights and would not materially affect the company’s daily operations. It remained uncertain whether actual control would shift, the company added.
According to another announcement back in March, Zhan said he planned to offload up to 2.9 per cent of the company’s total shares through centralised bidding and block trades between April 23 and July 22. It was intended to settle his share pledge loans and cover family financial needs. So far, the share reduction plan has not been carried out.
Dividing equity through the holdings of the controlling or largest shareholder following a divorce is not an uncommon practice either at home or abroad.
Globally, the biggest divorce settlement on record belongs to Jeff Bezos, who parted with Amazon shares worth US$38 billion to his ex-wife MacKenzie Scott in 2019.
In China, Zhou Hongyi, founder of US-sanctioned 360 Security Technology, finalised his divorce with Hu Huan in 2023. He transferred 6.25 per cent of his equity stake in the firm to his ex-wife, with the holding valued at nearly 9 billion yuan, according to the company.
In 2024, the China Securities Regulatory Commission issued new guidelines on share reduction by major shareholders. The rules closed loopholes that allowed indirect share reduction through divorce, securities lending, derivatives transactions and other ways.

data.title: ‘Rats everywhere’: numbers are up 323%. So why are authorities not worried?
data.description:
Hong Kong resident Yau Suk-ming was startled in her kitchen when her daughter turned on a countertop blender and a rat leapt out into the open.
The rodent scurried away before the 74-year-old retiree could catch it, but she was not surprised as she had earlier noticed rat droppings in her flat on Kowloon City’s Bulkeley Street.
“Turns out rats are afraid of ‘earthquakes’ – but I was not afraid, I am very proud of that,” she joked, saying she could not do much but put up with them.
Yau may be sanguine about her pest problem, but the rats lurking in the underbelly of the city’s wet markets, butcher shops and fresh produce stores have increasingly become a cause for wider concern about public health and hygiene.
The number of live or dead rodents caught and collected every year has risen by 323 per cent in the past decade, but authorities said their containment strategy, assisted by AI, was working.
That message, however, could contradict what residents saw in their daily lives, experts said.


“A high rodent absence rate gives the impression that Hong Kong is almost rodent-free in certain districts, yet some residents have reported that the rat infestation problem is getting worse,” said Brian Wong Shui-hung, a researcher at local think tank Liber Research Community.
“It is important to communicate the severity of the rodent situation effectively to the public, to enable people to understand the extent of the problem, track changes and act accordingly if needed.”
Tracking rats with AI
The Food and Environmental Hygiene Department (FEHD) said it had deployed the latest artificial intelligence technology to track and capture rodents.
Thermal imaging cameras installed across Hong Kong take images at two-minute intervals from 7pm to 7am for three consecutive nights, every six months.
The system’s AI algorithm detected and counted rats from their thermal signatures, analysed the frequency of their appearance and peak activity hours, and mapped dispersal routes, a spokesman told the South China Morning Post.
This provided the absence rate, which was measured by dividing the number of snapshots in which no rodents were detected by the total number of images taken.
According to department statistics, the rate exceeded 90 per cent across all 18 districts in the second half of last year. Eastern, Islands and Tuen Mun districts were more than 98 per cent rat-free, while Sai Kung topped the list at 99.5 per cent.
Wan Chai and Kowloon City were the only two districts with more than 10 locations recording a rat absence rate below 80 per cent.

To ensure dynamic surveillance that responded to new rat hotspots, the placement of the thermal cameras was “continuously calibrated” based on verified complaints, live and dead rodent catch figures, routine inspections and views of local communities, the spokesman said.
The number of live or dead rodents caught or collected in Hong Kong in the first half of 2026 stood at 87,105. The annual number had risen in the past decade, from 37,169 in 2016 to 157,182 in 2025 – a 323 per cent increase.


The number of rodent complaints had hovered at around 10,000 to 11,000 annually over the past decade. But the number received by the FEHD from January to June this year jumped by 33 per cent year on year to 6,463 – the highest in the past four years.
The rise in live rodents caught indicated an “improved operational efficiency” rather than evidence of a growing rodent population, the FEHD spokesman said, adding that the relatively stable number of complaints showed “no evidence of a territory-wide surge in rodent infestation”.
Does tracking work?
In June, authorities cracked down on the Kwun Tong branch of a popular butcher shop, Qiandama, after a video showing rats running over pig carcasses on the premises went viral online.
It was followed soon after by another viral video showing rats scurrying around Taste supermarket in Festival Walk shopping centre in Kowloon Tong.
A few months earlier, a rat was spotted in a sushi chiller at a Don Don Donki outlet in Tuen Mun.
“The rat problem in Hong Kong is so bad this year,” one user said on the social media site Threads. “Once it gets dark, you easily see a dozen rats in a single alleyway in the older districts.”


As more rodent sightings were shared online, questions arose about the accuracy of the thermal camera system as an indicator of Hong Kong’s rodent problem.
Wong from Liber Research Community said that while the absence rate was a “robust metric” to gauge rodent activity, it was ineffective as a means of communicating policy to the general public, whose experiences could contradict the positive figures.
Echoing this sentiment, Ming Wai-kit, an associate professor at City University’s department of infectious disease and public health, said: “While the rate provided by the government looks good, the reality is that the public’s perception is different. They actually see rats walking past them.”
Michael H Parsons, an urban ecologist and senior research scientist at the Centre for Urban Ecological Solutions in Texas, who has studied rodent issues in Singapore and Tokyo, noted that a high rodent absence rate did not mean Hong Kong was rodent-free.

It could simply mean the rats had shifted to other locations that were not under surveillance, he said.
One way to gauge the scale of a rodent problem was to measure sightings in the daytime over a few days, Parsons said.
“Daytime activity indicates extremely high population pressure … persistent daytime sightings [of rodents] would suggest an overgrown rodent population, regardless of what the rodent absence rate percentage suggests,” he said.
Ming, the City University epidemiologist, said global cities such as Toronto, New York, London, Paris and Amsterdam experienced rodent problems because of their high urban density, levels of food waste and ageing drainage facilities.

These cities tackled rodent problems by ensuring that water sources were clean, sealing off abandoned buildings and drainage channels where rats were located, and implementing surveillance measures and traps, he said.
Ming cited Tokyo’s approach to curbing rat infestations, including strong hygiene practices among the population, proper food waste categorisation and recycling.
To control rat levels in Hong Kong, authorities had to address illegal dumping and ensure food waste was handled properly, Ming said.
“Hong Kong’s rat problem is not very severe [compared with overseas cities] ... but certain areas or districts have more serious rodent infestations as they are located close to markets, housing estates and eateries,” he said.
Ming suggested that the government create an online reporting system so residents could register rat sightings and help the government measure and contain their prevalence.
According to FEHD data, the department caught 20,215 rodents in Yau Tsim Mong last year, up by nearly 39 per cent from 2024, and the highest number among the city’s 18 districts.
The increase was 26.4 per cent in Wong Tai Sin, 67.5 per cent in Sham Shui Po and 83.5 per cent in Kowloon City.
Kowloon City also registered 776 complaints in the first half of this year, nearly double the 396 recorded in the same period in 2025 and the highest number among districts.
District councillor Lam Tak-shing, who is also the chairman of the local food, environment and hygiene committee, attributed the rising trend to the large number of redevelopment projects in Kowloon City, including 65 construction sites.
The works and ensuing vibrations had sent more rats into the public, he noted, while adding that enforcement had also been stepped up.


In January, the FEHD ordered the closure of a butcher shop on Lok Shan Road due to “poor hygiene conditions and serious rodent infestation”.
The owner of Chan Hing Wong Foods, a nearby grocery store, said it had long been paying HK$700 (US$89) a month for a professional rodent prevention service.
Employee Yan Chan described the service, which conducts regular hygiene maintenance and lays rat poison, as “worthwhile”.
“The rats were everywhere. If we did not do this, they would eat or bite through our products, causing an even greater loss. We have to throw away items touched by rats,” she said.
Chan noted that authorities had ramped up efforts to tackle the problem since the temporary closure of the butcher shop. She observed that there were fewer rats now, but welcomed more follow-up and prevention measures.
Lam, the district councillor, said the Highways Department had repaired broken concrete and sewage pipes in some of the alleys off 8 Ma Tau Wai Road in an attempt to restrict the movement of rodents, and that the infestation in the district was “largely under control”.


What more can be done?
Lawrence Iu Chun-yip, executive director of think tank Civic Exchange, noted that the government had developed a “more integrated rodent control framework” across different departments and sectors.
“Food waste management is an essential part of rodent prevention,” Iu said, adding that there had been some improvement in this area.
He suggested that the government focus on sustained outcomes, such as measuring the time it took to clear a rat-infested area and observing whether the rodents returned after being eradicated.
Peter Leung Kwong-yuen, chairman of the Pest Control Personnel Association of Hong Kong, estimated that about 70 per cent of rodent issues involved private estates and shopping centres, based on complaints and feedback from the pest control industry.
He supported the government’s move to set up a charter targeting rat control in residential buildings.
In 2024, the FEHD launched the anti-rodent charter to encourage private stakeholders to step up rodent-control inspections and education. In April this year, it extended the charter to shopping centres.
Leung urged the government to step up contractor training and oversight of how workers handled the pests.


In July, some workers were caught keeping trapped rats and feeding them diced apples and sweet potatoes rather than exterminating them. The contractor was later suspended by the department.
Leung called the workers’ actions “inappropriate” and also criticised the industry’s inconsistent methods of controlling pests.
The FEHD spokesman said proactive inspections, law enforcement actions, health education campaigns and public complaints all helped to identify areas of infestation and prompt deployment of resources.
“The department calls on all stakeholders – property managers, food businesses, market operators and residents alike – to play their part,” he said.
The message has reached Yau, the Kowloon City retiree, who told the SCMP that she had recently started to clean more aggressively, wiping areas with bleach.

data.title: Alibaba unit says 5-in-1 AI gives robots unified brain, bodyABot framework addresses industry’s lack of a complete, self-evolving system to improve robot capabilities, according to Amap.
data.description:
Alibaba Group Holding’s mapping unit has unveiled an upgrade to what it calls the world’s first technology framework that unites a robot’s “feet, hands, brains, central nerves and motor nerves” into a single system.
Amap’s ABot system marks the latest move in Chinese tech players’ race to harness artificial intelligence models to make robots more capable. Referred to as embodied intelligence, the effort aims to equip machines with the “brains” and other elements required to navigate and complete tasks in complex physical spaces.
With five new specialised foundation AI models working together, ABot aimed to address a systemic problem in the industry, according to Amap: the lack of a complete, self-evolving system.
“The industry has long focused on breakthroughs in single models – better navigation models, more powerful operation models and smarter reasoning models – but these models operate independently without shared data and experience,” it said in a social media post on Wednesday.
With its five models – ABot-N1, ABot-M0.5, ABot-ER, ABot-AgentOS, and ABot-C0 – working in concert, the Amap system sought to shatter those silos, according to the firm.
The ABot-N1 model employed an “eyes guiding feet” approach, enabling a robot to handle city-scale autonomous navigation with a 92.9 per cent outdoor success rate using only standard cameras and basic road networks, Amap said on Wednesday, adding that this bypassed the need for costly high-definition maps.
Meanwhile, ABot-M0.5 allowed a machine to “use hands and feet simultaneously”, divorcing movement functions from manipulation tasks so a robot could seamlessly grasp objects while walking, the company said.
ABot-ER and ABot-AgentOS acted as brain and central nervous system, respectively, helping to link tasks to objects, and then adapting across heterogeneous robotic bodies, according to Amap. ABot-C0 served as the “motor nerves” that finally translated the decisions of the ABot system into robot actions, it said.

The framework had secured state-of-the-art results across 17 global robotics benchmarks, the company added.
Tencent Holdings launched its own full-stack embodied intelligence offering at the latest World AI Conference in Shanghai last week, debuting its Hy-Embodied vision-language-action models to help robots perceive and reason.
Robbyant, the embodied AI arm of Chinese fintech giant Ant Group, earlier this month launched LingBot-Depth 2.0, its next-generation spatial perception model, which was designed to help robots accurately perceive glass, mirrors and transparent objects.
Ant is the fintech affiliate of Alibaba Group Holding, which also owns the South China Morning Post.
Amap, an increasingly important player in Alibaba’s AI push, has been pushing deeper into embodied intelligence in recent months. It also stepped up research into so-called world models – systems designed to simulate real-world environments to train robots to understand the physical world.

data.title: AI experts blast US claims of ‘IP theft’ by Moonshot’s Kimi K3
data.description:
The global AI community has pushed back against the Trump administration’s claims that Moonshot AI’s groundbreaking Kimi K3 distilled US models, citing a lack of justification and arguing that the practice does not constitute intellectual property theft.
In separate posts on X on Thursday, a number of AI experts called the accusations “political” and “reckless”, pointing out that an AI model’s output was not copyrighted.
A Moonshot employee on Thursday hit back at the US officials’ claims that the Chinese company conducted “large-scale, covert industrial distillation” against US models including Anthropic’s cutting-edge Fable 5, by pointing out the narrow window between the release of Kimi K3 and Fable 5.
“Yes, Fable went public on July 1 and K3 launched on July 15. We trained a brand new frontier model in JUST 15 DAYS. Guinness World Record stuff,” Moonshot AI employee Randy Xian wrote on X.
While US officials and AI labs have long attributed Chinese AI’s rapid advancement to distillation, a common industry practice of using the outputs of larger, more capable models to train smaller ones, the White House adviser on Wednesday escalated the accusations by describing the practice as “stealing proprietary US technology”.
Michael Kratsios, US President Donald Trump’s science and technology adviser, made the accusation on X on Wednesday. Then US Under Secretary of State Jacob Helberg echoed Kratsios’ remarks, characterising Moonshot AI’s alleged distillation efforts as “a heist of invaluable American Intellectual Property”.
Anthropic announced Claude Fable 5 in early June, with built-in “safeguards” that downgraded a range of requests, including distillation efforts, to the older model Claude Opus 4.8. The company then took Fable 5 offline following Washington’s directive to block all foreign nationals, before re-releasing it on July 1.
Beijing-based Moonshot did not publicly respond to the latest accusations from Washington and did not immediately respond to a request for comment on Thursday.

Researchers also pointed to the lack of evidence for any distillation claims, while some highlighted Kimi K3’s technical innovations.
It is generally difficult to prove whether a large language model is distilled from another model, and there is currently insufficient evidence to prove if Kimi K3 was distilled from Fable 5, according to Michael Chau, professor in Innovation and Information Management at the University of Hong Kong Business School.
In a Wednesday post on X, Stephen Hsu, professor of Computational Mathematics, Science, and Engineering at Michigan State University, called it “lazy and simplistic” to attribute K3’s advances to distillation of US models.
The Kimi model’s breakthrough “lies in fundamentally re-architecting the model’s computational paradigm through innovations”, according to Hsu, citing the Kimi K3 paper, which mentioned mechanisms such as Kimi Delta Attention and Attention Residuals, “architectural updates designed to improve how information flows across sequence length and model depth”.
Huang Zhenxin, Moonshot AI’s head of enterprise business, was quoted as saying earlier this week by Chinese media outlets that Kimi K3 was “not a distillation or replica of existing models”, and that its performance leap stemmed from “underlying original architectural innovation”. Huang made the comments to local media before Washington’s accusations.
Others criticised the Trump administration’s attempt to categorise distillation as intellectual property theft.
An AI model’s output or chain of thought is not copyrighted nor treated as trade secrets, Kevin Xu, founder of Interconnected Capital.
“Thus, alleging distillation as ‘IP theft’ is not a serious legal argument. It is a political and lobbying talking point masquerading as a legal argument,” Xu said.
US Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent on Thursday threatened action against Chinese AI firms, saying on X that “sanctions and Entity List designations will be on the table” when distillation attacks “cross the line into intellectual property theft”.
Bessent’s threat “appears to be a bit reckless”, as the justification for using sanctions in this case remained “quite unclear”, Paul Triolo, partner and technology policy lead at DGA-Albright Stonebridge Group.
As more US businesses look to open-weight alternatives amid soaring AI spending, Silicon Valley start-ups called for the Trump administration not to restrict access to Chinese open-weight AI.
“Denying American start-ups access to models available abroad would stifle competition, entrench incumbents, and function as a tax on intelligence, raising their costs and narrowing their choices while foreign competitors retain access to the full global market,” 179 US firms, including prominent start-up incubator Y Combinator, said in a letter addressed to Kratsios and Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, and obtained by Politico.
Instead of penalising Moonshot’s Kimi models, restricting Kimi access in the US penalises “all American consumers and enterprises”, Xiaoyin Qu, founder of US AI agent firm Tycoon AI, wrote on social media on Thursday.
Such a move would increase American companies’ AI operational spending and slow down AI adoption in the US, she said.
